title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text: The objective of this project is to build an interactive, visually stunning webpage using Quarto (.qmd) that analyzes Tesla’s (TSLA) stock history from its IPO in 2010 to the present. We will use Python for data processing and visualization, aiming to create educational and interesting graphics that reveal trends, volatility, and trading patterns.

title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text:
Phase 1: Data Acquisition & Directory Setup

Create project structure (code/, downloads/).
Implement robust multi-source downloader that handles fallback options (Nasdaq API to Yahoo Finance).
Fetch full TSLA history from IPO (June 29, 2010) to current date (tsla_all_historical.csv).

title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text:
Phase 2: Data Exploration & Feature Engineering
Before creating graphics, we need to enrich the dataset with technical indicators that make for interesting visualizations: * Moving Averages (MA): * Short-term: 20-day and 50-day Simple Moving Average (SMA). * Long-term: 200-day SMA (crucial for detecting Golden Crosses and Death Crosses). * Volatilities & Returns: * Daily percentage return: \(R_t = \frac{P_t - P_{t-1}}{P_{t-1}}\) * 30-day rolling volatility (Standard Deviation of returns). * Trading Bands: * Bollinger Bands (20-day SMA \(\pm\) 2 standard deviations) to show overbought/oversold levels.
#| label: feature-engineering
#| eval: false
#| echo: true
# Example code block for indicators:
import pandas as pd

df = pd.read_csv("../downloads/tsla_all_historical.csv")
df['date'] = pd.to_datetime(df['date'])

# Moving averages
df['sma_50'] = df['close'].rolling(window=50).mean()
df['sma_200'] = df['close'].rolling(window=200).mean()

# Daily Returns
df['daily_return'] = df['close'].pct_change()

title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text:
Phase 3: Interactive Visualizations
We will design at least four key interactive graphics using libraries like Plotly or Altair to allow zooming, panning, and hovering:

1. Candlestick & Moving Average Overlay

Visuals: Candlesticks (Open, High, Low, Close) overlaid with 50-day and 200-day SMAs, plus a bar chart of volume at the bottom.
Goal: Understand long-term trends and identify support/resistance levels.



2. Bollinger Bands Deviation Chart

Visuals: Close price plotted alongside upper, middle, and lower Bollinger Bands with shaded bounds.
Goal: Highlight periods of high volatility (squeezes) and potential price reversals.



3. Daily Returns & Volatility Clustering

Visuals: Dual-axis chart showing daily returns as a scatter/line plot and rolling 30-day volatility as an area chart.
Goal: Visualize volatility clustering (the phenomenon where high-volatility days are followed by high-volatility days).



4. Cumulative Returns & Drawdown Analysis

Visuals: Peak-to-trough drop (%) line plot showing the maximum paper loss an investor would face during major corrections.
Goal: Learn about investment risk, recovery times, and the scale of market corrections (e.g., 2022 bear market).

title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text:
Phase 4: Webpage Assembly & Styling
Quarto allows us to render .qmd files into highly customized HTML documents: - Theme Selection: Select a modern theme (such as slate or cosmo, customized with custom CSS for dark mode/glassmorphism). - Responsive Layout: Grid systems to arrange charts side-by-side or stacked on mobile devices. - Code Folding: Keep the technical Python code tucked away in collapsible blocks (code-fold: true) so readers can focus on the graphics but easily inspect the source code if they choose.

title: Tesla (TSLA) Historical Stock Data Analysis
text:
Phase 5: Rendering & Publishing

Render the document locally to a single self-contained HTML page:
quarto render project_outline.qmd --to html
Make it hostable via GitHub Pages, Netlify, or local distribution.

title: Market Performance & Concentration Dashboard
section: Market Concentration: Magnificent Seven vs. Broad Market (Base: Jan 2020 = 100%)
text:
Market Concentration: Magnificent Seven vs. Broad Market (Base: Jan 2020 = 100%)




M7 vs RSP Mega-Cap Tech Concentration vs. Equal-Weight S&P 500 | Comparing tech giants to the average stock


⛶ Full Screen




                            
                                            







Understanding the Indices



Magnificent Seven Index (Equal Weight): Tracks the equal-weighted average performance of the market’s leading mega-cap tech giants: Microsoft (MSFT), Apple (AAPL), NVIDIA (NVDA), Alphabet (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Tesla (TSLA).


S&P 500 ETF (SPY): The standard market-cap-weighted benchmark. In this structure, larger companies exert greater influence over the index’s direction, reflecting the performance of the US market weighted heavily by its largest components.


S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP): Allocates an equal weighting to all 500 companies in the S&P 500. This represents the performance of the “average stock” and strips away the dominant impact of mega-cap leaders.



Market Health & Future Outlook


This chart highlights a historic divergence: the Magnificent Seven has outpaced the equal-weight market by over 700% since 2020. This indicates extreme market concentration, where broad index gains are heavily reliant on a small group of tech giants. For the future, this level of concentration represents both an opportunity and a risk:



Systemic Concentration Risk: Because the standard S&P 500 (SPY) is cap-weighted, any weakness or correction in the Magnificent Seven will disproportionately drag down the entire broad market index, even if the remaining 493 stocks remain stable.


Breadth Catch-up Opportunity: A healthy, long-term bull market relies on broad participation. If economic indicators stabilize, capital may rotate out of highly valued tech leaders and spread into the broader market, allowing RSP to catch up to SPY. Alternatively, a market-wide correction may occur if the growth expectations for these seven mega-cap tech giants fail to materialize.
